text: Complex computational systems that transcend traditional boundaries, operating on real numbers and capable of processing a vast array of functions beyond the realm of general recursion, inherently rely on an underlying framework of infinite information, often manifesting as an arbitrary non-computable value or unconventional procedural approach. This research shows that while Turing universality can be achieved at specific Turing degrees, it proves elusive across all degrees, rendering universality conceptually sound at the most fundamental level but increasingly problematic as complexity escalates. To address this conundrum, we propose the concept of universal relativity, accompanied by a novel framework for bridging successive tiers within both the arithmetical and analytical hierarchies.
label: ai

text: In pursuit of a comprehensive understanding of computation and its relationship with computing devices, this research endeavors to anchor the concept in the capacity of systems to adaptively interact with their external environment. By quantifying this adaptability through a programmability metric, the goal is to broaden the scope of applicability across diverse domains, encompassing not only digital machinery but also biological and natural phenomena. Building upon an earlier-developed phase transition coefficient, this approach generalizes its application to describe the dynamic properties of cellular automata and comparable systems, effectively gauging a system's ability to respond to external stimuli and be programmable.
label: ai

text: In a radical departure from traditional approaches, computability logic redefines logic as an inquiry into the fundamental principles of computation itself, rather than solely as a study of truth. This innovative framework views formulas as interactive computational puzzles, where logical connectives serve as transformations that manipulate these problems, and truth is reinterpreted as the availability of a computable solution. As research unfolds, the theoretical foundation of CL remains adaptable and open to expansion. Scholars have thus far explored three distinct varieties of conjunction and disjunction operators—parallel, sequential, and choice—and this work introduces a fourth, toggling, which relaxes the choice operation, permitting reversals and reconsideration up to a finite limit. Essentially, toggling captures iterative, experimental approaches to decision-making within machine-environment interactions. This study presents a comprehensive and accurate proof system for the basic propositional layer of CL, employing a language that incorporates negation alongside all four conjunction and disjunction varieties—parallel, toggling, sequential, and choice. Simultaneously, it develops toggling versions of quantification and iterative operations.
label: ai

text:
In Shannon's seminal work, he posited that an omnipotent attacker would inevitably obtain any transmitted information, setting the stage for modern cryptography. Later, Diffie and Hellman refined this threat model, acknowledging the practical constraints faced by real-world adversaries. This paradigm shift revolutionized computer science, ultimately giving rise to contemporary cryptographic practices.

Furthermore, Shannon also considered adversaries with unlimited logical and observational capabilities, encapsulated in the notion that the adversary comprehends the system. This perspective has been perpetuated in cryptography, with Kerckhoffs' principle asserting that obscuring the system does not provide genuine protection. In essence, encryption methods cannot remain hidden, and security must rely on confidential keys. Modern cryptography surpasses both Shannon and Kerckhoffs by assuming that if an exploitable algorithm exists, the adversary will inevitably discover it. Rather than depicting the attacker as an all-powerful machine, contemporary theory portrays the attacker as an infinitely skilled programmer.

Notably, the shift from unlimited to constrained computational resources, as seen in the Diffie-Hellman model, has not been mirrored by a similar shift in the representation of logical or programming resources. Does assuming that adversaries will eventually discover and deploy any feasible algorithm fundamentally differ from presuming that all computable functions will ultimately be executed? This study aims to explore novel approaches to enhance current representations of attackers and defenders by acknowledging their finite logical and programming capabilities. Can a system achieve enhanced robustness by dynamically adapting to the adversary's tactics and modifying its defenses accordingly, as the adversary continuously probes the system to identify weaknesses?
label: ai

text: In the realm of formal systems, Computability Logic (CL) provides a rigorous framework for understanding computational processes and their corresponding costs. Building upon its adaptable linguistic structure, CL can serve as a solid foundation for various applied disciplines and technological platforms that require logically sound and computationally meaningful underpinnings. One notable subset of CL's linguistic resources is Heyting's intuitionistic calculus INT, which, despite its constructivist orientation, has lacked a formalized semantic foundation until now. CL offers the potential to rectify this shortcoming and fulfill Kolmogorov's vision of INT as the "logic of problems". This study demonstrates the soundness of INT within the CL semantics. For more information on CL, visit http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html
label: ai

text: In this research, a novel formal system, dubbed ptarithmetic, is introduced, which draws inspiration from Peano arithmetic but is grounded in the cutting-edge concept of computability logic. Unlike traditional number theory frameworks, ptarithmetic views formulas as problem representations rather than mere statements with truth values. The validity of a formula is equivalent to the existence of a polynomial-time algorithm capable of solving the problem it represents. The ptarithmetic framework presented here showcases both soundness and completeness. Soundness ensures that every theorem T encodes a polynomial-time resolvable problem in number theory, and its resolution can be derived effectively from T's proof. Completeness guarantees that any problem admitting a polynomial-time solution corresponds to a theorem within the system. This work is self-contained and does not rely on prior knowledge of computability logic.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking approach, Computability Logic (CoL) seeks to revolutionize classical logic by replacing truth with computability within its formal semantics, as explored on the University of Pennsylvania's Computer and Information Science website (http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html). The article lays the groundwork for CoL by establishing a solid foundation in complexity theory, followed by a demonstration of the soundness and completeness of the deductive system CL12 relative to CoL's semantics, encompassing both general computability and polynomial-time computable functions. CL12 is formulated as a sequent calculus, where a sequent is read as "the right-hand side can be algorithmically reduced to the left-hand side." The system's formulas consist of predicate symbols, function symbols, variables, constants, equality, negation, conjunction and disjunction, and existential and universal quantifiers, along with choice operators. The article posits that CL12 provides a suitable logical framework for applied constructive theories, particularly those focused on computational complexity.
label: ai

text: In the realm of quantum computing, algorithms are comprised of abstract sequences of operations that run on hypothetical machines. A categorical framework is crucial for accurately conceptualizing these algorithms. Building upon the work of Abramsky, Coecke, and Selinger, this research contributes fresh perspectives to the field. Specifically, we delve into the function abstraction mechanism within quantum computation, uncovering its link to classical computational limits. Certain quantum algorithms offer practical solutions to notoriously complex problems, such as integer factorization and the discrete logarithm problem, which underpin modern cryptographic systems. Understanding the computational resources necessary to execute these quantum algorithms has far-reaching practical implications. Despite multiple approaches to physically implementing quantum computers, determining the necessary conditions remains an ongoing challenge. Categorical semantics offers valuable instruments for tackling such fundamental questions. We demonstrate that a significant subset of quantum algorithms can be implemented using only abelian groups and their relational structures.
label: ai

text: Two pressing questions have long remained underscrutinized, begging for a robust and systematic theoretical approach. Specifically, we seek to understand what drives computational endeavors and which problems necessitate computational solutions. The hurdle lies not in the scarcity of answers, but rather in the absence of a unifying theoretical framework capable of synthesizing them in a coherent and comprehensive manner. Our purpose in posing these queries is to stimulate thoughtful examination and exploration into these areas, ultimately seeking to foster a deeper understanding.
label: ai

text: Computability logic is a novel, informal framework that has revolutionized the way we approach computational abilities and problems. Emerging relatively recently, this non-traditional logical system has managed to conserve the fundamental principles of classical first-order logic while exponentially increasing its expressive capacity, paving the way for a plethora of innovative applications. Interestingly, this framework bears a striking resemblance to intuitionistic and linear logics, which share a similar property of extending classical logic while offering enhanced expressiveness. As such, computability logic can be seen as a harmonious fusion of three prominent logical schools of thought, seamlessly integrating their principles and perspectives within its comprehensive, game-theoretic framework. For further exploration, readers are directed to a comprehensive resource available at http://www.csc.villanova.edu/~japaridz/CL/.
label: ai

text:
In modern computing systems, a von Neumann architecture is widely employed, dividing memory into separate sections for instructions and data. Similarly, future quantum technologies, encompassing computation, communication, and data management, may benefit from analogous architectural designs. Quantum information science theories raise intriguing questions about experimentally validating programmable quantum systems, despite progress being made. However, fundamental quantum mechanics constraints introduce significant challenges. To overcome these, two primary approaches have emerged: stochastic and deterministic methods. This research focuses on the latter, referred to as programmable quantum networks operating with pure states.

This study delves into core concepts and theoretical frameworks relevant to engineering microscale systems, including conditional quantum evolution, the Nielsen-Chuang "no-programming theorem, and arrays of deterministic and stochastic quantum gates. The exploration covers both finite-register programmable quantum networks and hybrid systems utilizing continuous quantum variables. The chosen architectural framework for a universal programmable quantum network with pure states and a finite program register is the "Control-Shift" quantum processor design, which incorporates three communication channels developed previously. Furthermore, it is shown that quantum cellular automata-based approaches to constructing universal programmable quantum computers often represent special cases of this architectural framework.
label: ai

text: Within the paradigm of default logic, Reiter's formulation permits the application of a default rule even when it contradicts a previously invoked default rule, resulting in what is known as a failure. Traditionally, this phenomenon has been regarded as a semantic obstacle, prompting the creation of alternative formulations aimed at circumventing it. Conversely, we argue that failures offer a crucial computational advantage, enabling the representation of certain problem domains within the default logic framework.
label: ai

text:
Instead of treating logic as a study of truth, computability logic reimagines it as a mathematical inquiry into the workings of computational processes. Here, formulas represent interactive computational tasks, pitting a machine against its environment; logical operations reshape these computational entities; and truth is redefined as the availability of an algorithmic solution – a winning strategy or algorithm for the machine.

The framework remains adaptable, open to refinement as research progresses. CL research has so far focused on four operator categories: parallel, choice, branching, and blind. This work introduces a novel operator category, sequential, which encompasses sequential conjunction and disjunction, sequential quantification systems, and iteration constructs. This category reflects the intuitive computational model: sequential operations unfold as a step-by-step process, unlike the parallel model associated with the parallel operator group. To execute a sequential combination of tasks requires tackling each component one at a time, proceeding from start to finish.

The main technical contribution of this work establishes a robust and comprehensive formal system for the propositional component of computability logic, comprising the operator vocabulary – alongside standard negation – with three conjunction and disjunction variants: parallel, choice, and sequential. A parallel advancement is also presented for the first-order case.
label: ai

text: In the digital realm, a computer program refers to the sequence of instructions that a machine executes within its memory to perform specific tasks. These instructions, processed by the central processing unit, dictate the computer's behavior and enable it to accomplish various computational goals. Humans craft computer programs using programming languages, which establish the rules and conventions necessary for composing these instructions. Interestingly, most programming languages have their roots in English-speaking regions, resulting in English serving as the primary foundation for their syntax and vocabulary. However, alternative languages have emerged from non-English linguistic contexts, such as the Arabic Loughaty, Russian Rapira, and Chinese variants of BASIC and Python. This project introduces Phoenix, a pioneering programming language built upon Arabic as its fundamental linguistic basis. Designed exclusively for Arabic-speaking users, Phoenix is a versatile, high-level, imperative, object-oriented, and compiled language. Its architecture is centered around six core modules: a preprocessor, scanner, parser, semantic analyzer, code generator, and linker. Through rigorous testing, Phoenix has demonstrated its ability to execute complex tasks, such as function definitions, while-loops, and mathematical calculations. Future development plans aim to enhance Phoenix's capabilities by incorporating advanced features like polymorphism, inheritance, networking, graphical user interfaces, and file management operations.
label: ai

text:
Computers play a crucial role in automating various processes, thereby minimizing human intervention in routine tasks. Over the years, automation has consistently proved to be a cost-effective and efficient means of reducing operational expenses, streamlining maintenance, and enhancing output quality and dependability. Today, most automation systems rely on software programs that run within a computer's memory and processor to regulate multiple functions.

This project introduces a compiler-based tool designed to streamline the conversion of Arabic-language documents into structured XML files that machines can process. The input material is typically disorganized plain text created manually by users, whereas the output is a formatted, computer-readable XML structure. This compiler is part of a broader initiative aimed at building an e-government infrastructure to expedite the management and preservation of legal documents and records. The compiler consists of three key components: a lexical analyzer, a syntactic analyzer, and a translation module. Experimental results indicate that implementing automation techniques can serve as a foundation for creating an e-government system tailored to Lebanon's administrative needs. Future phases of development will focus on examining additional categories of legal documents that require validation mechanisms and remediation procedures.
label: ai

text: As the discipline of image processing has evolved to aid in the management and organization of visual content, a pressing challenge has emerged: the overwhelming volume of pictorial data generated by omnipresent imaging technologies, expansive digital networks, and multimedia platforms now outstrips human capacity for processing. To mitigate this issue, computational systems have become indispensable collaborators, necessitating intelligent capabilities that mirror human cognitive abilities. The Cognitive Computing movement, which has gained significant traction in recent years, is poised to address this need by focusing on cognitive image processing. Notably, this development is part of a broader paradigm shift unfolding across multiple scientific, technological, and industrial spheres, marked by a transition from data-centric to information-centric approaches. While this transformation holds great promise, it faces significant hurdles stemming from the ambiguity surrounding the distinction between data and information. This work seeks to clarify these foundational questions through an examination of cognitive image processing.
label: ai

text: Within the theoretical framework of computer science, the notion of NP complexity has far-reaching implications. In parallel, researchers have endeavored to define its quantum analogue, dubbed Quantum NP. Notably, two distinct approaches have emerged: NQP, proposed by Adleman, DeMarrais, and Huang, and QMA, developed by Knill, Kitaev, and Watrous. Through the lens of operator theory, NP arises when the exists-operator is applied to P. Building upon this foundation, Green, Homer, Moore, and Pollett have introduced the N-operator, which encapsulates the essence of NQP. Furthermore, the authors have generalized QMA, introducing the exists^{Q}-operator and its dual, the forall^{Q}-operator. These constructs not only shed light on Quantum NP but also establish a hierarchical structure within the quantum realm, echoing the Meyer-Stockmeyer polynomial hierarchy and rooted in the principles of two-sided bounded-error quantum computation.
label: ai

text: Despite being the cornerstone of game theory, Nash equilibrium faces significant challenges, particularly in repeated prisoner's dilemma scenarios where its practical applicability and descriptive accuracy are questionable. Furthermore, when viewed through the prism of computer science, the concept's fragility in response to unusual player behavior, lack of mechanisms for addressing coalitional dynamics, failure to account for computational costs, and inability to accommodate incomplete knowledge of the game's structure become evident. In light of these limitations, alternative approaches and refinements have been developed to overcome the shortcomings of the Nash equilibrium framework.
label: ai

text:
In the realm of Theoretical Computer Science, Stone duality serves as a powerful mathematical framework that converges disparate areas of research, including Domain Theory, concurrent systems, and program logics. Developed by pioneers such as Scott, Milner, and Hennessy, this unifying concept harmonizes denotational semantics and operational semantics, bridging the gap between computational processes and their logical representations.

Stone duality reveals a profound connection between semantics, understood as domains of points denoting computational processes, and logics, envisioned as lattices encoding process properties. Crucially, this connection is rooted in geometric principles, yielding a logic of detectable properties – characteristics verifiable through finite observational data from a process's execution.

This foundational insight inspires a novel approach:

Firstly, a metalanguage is crafted, comprising:

* Types, which serve as domains of discourse for describing various computational phenomena
* Terms, which represent programs as syntactic models or points

Next, the metalanguage receives dual interpretations:

* Denotational interpretation: types are assigned domains, and terms are mapped to domain values
* Logical interpretation: types correspond to propositional theories, and terms are explicated through a program logic that formalizes their defining properties

Finally, the dual interpretations are connected through Stone duality, ensuring mutual consistency and equivalence up to isomorphism. This framework opens the door to numerous practical applications. Starting with a denotational characterization of a computational phenomenon, one can mechanically derive the corresponding logic for analyzing that phenomenon.
label: ai

text: As businesses adapt to the digital landscape, they are reorienting their operational frameworks around service-centric architectures to harmonize distribution, marketing, and collaborative networks, while streamlining infrastructure development and maintenance to minimize costs. Despite this shift, the emerging paradigm is still lacking crucial components necessary to establish robust and adaptable technological foundations that can swiftly respond to dynamic commercial and organizational changes. This article proposes a novel approach to building Ecosystem-Oriented Architectures (EOA) grounded in digital service frameworks. The core of this approach consists of a multi-faceted ecosystem layer comprising computational elements designed to ensure seamless interoperability, effortless data exchange, intelligent oversight, autonomous connectivity, self-modifying capabilities, and security across all linked services, modules, and components within the framework. Ultimately, this framework aims to provide a holistic and versatile enterprise technology platform for constructing agile digital organizations that continuously meet shifting market demands, emerging trends, and evolving expectations. Future research could further enhance the presented framework by integrating machine intelligence capabilities to facilitate analytical reasoning and problem-solving.
label: ai

text: When multiple agents work together to make collective decisions, a straightforward approach often prevails in coordinating their choices. A common method involves soliciting preference statements from all parties and then using voting procedures to determine the outcome. However, a significant issue arises when individuals intentionally distort their stated preferences to sway the results. Research has shown that detecting such strategic manipulation is a computationally difficult task across various voting mechanisms, classified as NP-hard. While worst-case complexity bounds offer some reassurance, theoretical studies suggest that manipulation is often feasible under typical circumstances. To bridge this gap between theory and reality, I propose a practical investigation into whether computational difficulty actually hinders manipulation attempts. The core of this research involves analyzing "phase transitions" – a technique proven effective in identifying intractable instances in satisfiability problems and related NP-complete challenges. My findings reveal that phase transitions shed light on the difficulty landscape surrounding voting rule manipulation, casting doubt on whether computational barriers provide meaningful protection. Furthermore, I explore the computational aspects of manipulation in connected domains such as stable matching problems and tournament structures.
label: ai

text:
Over the past seven decades, computing capabilities have witnessed an extraordinary surge, doubling approximately every five years, giving rise to what we now refer to as the digital revolution. This rapid progress has profoundly influenced the scientific landscape, particularly within the natural sciences, as computational modeling has become an integral part of research. Two methodologies have emerged as cornerstones of this paradigm shift: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) and Molecular Dynamics (MD).

This study delves into the MCMC framework, commencing with an overview of foundational simulation methods and statistical tools used to evaluate their efficacy. The discussion then shifts towards more advanced techniques: generalized ensembles and biased sampling schemes. These approaches hold immense potential for biomolecular research, with the prospect of gaining significant relevance in the near future. Special emphasis is placed on two notable methods – the multicanonical ensemble and the replica exchange method, also referred to as parallel tempering or multiple Markov chain methodology – which have the potential to significantly impact scientific discovery.
label: ai

text:
Rational decision-making is the cornerstone of game theory, which transcends human agents to encompass all living entities, from biological communities to genetic elements, as they interact with their environments. Rather than solely relying on deliberate calculation, rational conduct often arises organically through the natural tendency towards stable equilibrium states.

Formal theories of rational behavior are often grounded in informal observations drawn from real-world systems, such as economic markets, biological ecosystems, or interconnected networks. By applying computational principles to these systems, we can distill fundamental concepts of equilibrium and rationality and refine them further through algebraic and coalgebraic frameworks.

In game theory, the appearance of equilibrium points is well-established, but what's crucial is understanding how computational methods can unlock new approaches to analyzing equilibrium behavior. By doing so, we can develop innovative strategies for navigating complex systems and optimizing outcomes.
label: ai

text: Despite the theoretical NP-hardness of finding a winning manipulation strategy for various electoral systems, practical challenges may be more manageable due to the discovery of more feasible approaches in real-world scenarios. In this study, we empirically examine the cost of manipulating outcomes under the single transferable vote (STV) system, which, surprisingly, is one of the most resistant to manipulation among the computationally intractable electoral systems. STV's iterative elimination process and the fact that individual agent manipulation, without vote weighting or information about others' choices, renders it NP-hard, make it a particularly challenging case to crack. In nearly all our test elections, we found that determining either a single agent's manipulation strategy or confirming its infeasibility was computationally easy. However, the problem of coordinated manipulation by multiple agents remains an open question, posing a genuine computational challenge in practice.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking departure from traditional logic, computability logic (CoL) has revolutionized the field by anchoring it in the principles of computability, rather than mere truth. This paradigm shift enables logical statements to be reimagined as interactive computational processes, pitting computing systems against their environment in a competitive game-like framework. The concept of truth is redefined as the existence of a computable method to achieve victory in these games. CoL's logical connectives are now seen as transformations that operate on these games, allowing for a flexible and continually refined architecture. Building upon this foundation, the present work innovatively expands CoL's expressive capabilities, transcending the limitations of prior variants by introducing cirquents – network-like constructs inspired by circuit design. Cirquents bring a significant advantage: they permit the representation of overlapping subgames and subtasks shared across multiple components of a unified computational framework, yielding substantial benefits, particularly the ability to encompass, strengthen, and extend independence-friendly logic, which emerges as a natural subset of CoL. This work is self-contained and requires no prior knowledge of CoL.
label: ai

text: Within the realm of clarithmetic, a branch of computability logic, formulas function as representations of computational problems that can be solved algorithmically. The notion of truth is tied to the existence of an algorithmic resolution. Variations of clarithmetic arise from different constraints governing computational complexity. This paper introduces a parameterized variant, CLA11(P1,P2,P3,P4), which can be fine-tuned through three core parameters P1, P2, and P3. Adjusting these parameters yields automatically both sound and complete systems across various complexity categories, including temporal, spatial, and amplitude complexity. The soundness property ensures that every theorem corresponds to a problem with a solution satisfying the specified complexity constraints, and that solution can be mechanically derived from the proof. Completeness guarantees that every problem admitting a solution within the given complexity bounds is captured by a system theorem. Furthermore, adjusting parameter P4 enables intensional completeness, where every formula encoding a solvable problem within the complexity class is a theorem. However, this comes at the cost of sacrificing recursive axiomatizability while preserving conceptual clarity and aesthetic appeal. This paper is divided into two parts. Part I established the system and demonstrated its completeness, while Part II, presented here, focuses on establishing its soundness.
label: ai

text:
Mathematica is a cutting-edge computational powerhouse, widely applied across an array of scientific disciplines, from quantum computing to biological sciences and beyond. Its versatility stems from its comprehensive toolkit, which empowers users to craft code using procedural, functional, and rule-based approaches, or any blend of these paradigms. Seamlessly integrating with external applications via MathLink, a protocol facilitating communication between Mathematica processes and external software written in languages such as C, Java, and others, Mathematica excels in manipulating visual, mathematical, and document content.

This exploration delves into strategies for dispersing Mathematica computations across networked machines to expedite processing times. By partitioning program components across multiple computers, substantial calculations can be completed significantly faster, thereby enhancing overall computational performance. The parallelization framework is compatible with all Mathematica releases and operates harmoniously regardless of version discrepancies across networked nodes, functioning reliably on major platforms including Unix, Windows, and Macintosh environments. Although our investigation focuses specifically on Unix-based systems, the presented approach proves equally effective across alternative operating systems.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking departure, we develop a computational framework rooted in monoidal categories, adhering to the Church-Turing Thesis and encompassing the same range of computable functions as conventional approaches. This framework presents a unifying categorical perspective on most existing models, stripping away implementation-specific details through concepts and techniques born from recent breakthroughs in computational semantics and language design. One of the key benefits of monoidal category theory lies in its association with a precise and exhaustive graphical representation system—string diagrams—that enables intuitive analysis of computational processes. Our work is guided by a vision to create an accessible higher-level programming formalism capable of handling computational resource theory, including constructs such as one-way and trapdoor functions, by harnessing proven methods for obscuring implementation-level complexity. This paper marks an initial milestone toward this objective and outlines a strategic plan for its realization, with three forthcoming companion papers continuing along this trajectory.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking study, we employ bidirectional quantum finite automata to explore the efficiency of quantum computation, delving into the recognition of languages through the examination of unique behavioral patterns and fundamental structural characteristics. Notably, our findings reveal that when such automata conclude their computation within a finite number of operations across all possible paths, the time complexity is linear in the worst-case scenario. Moreover, for scenarios involving bounded error probability, the automata's language acceptance decisions rely exclusively on paths that complete within an exponential number of steps, regardless of whether all paths ultimately terminate. We also develop a method to simulate these automata using conventional two-way multi-head probabilistic finite automata equipped with cut points. Furthermore, we investigate how the automata's language recognition capabilities change when their acceptance conditions transition between error-free, unilateral error, bounded error, and unrestricted error configurations, highlighting the varying computational complexity characteristics of each variant. Finally, we observe that two-way unbounded-error and two-way bounded-error 2-head quantum finite automata, with arbitrary complex transition amplitudes, can recognize certain non-computable languages, whereas two-way error-free quantum finite automata are limited to recognizing only computable languages.
label: ai

text: In order to derive the full partition function of a modified Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model, we employ path integrals to describe the motion of a particle within an oscillator bath. Our investigation focuses on the impact of temperature-dependent electronic correlations, exploring the effects of varying oscillator frequencies on the system. By examining the average trajectory of the particle over time, we uncover modifications to the harmonic partition function and free energy that are sensitive to temperature. This approach enables the calculation of total heat capacity up to ambient temperatures, revealing an increased heat capacity-to-temperature ratio at low temperatures indicative of glass-like behavior. This phenomenon is attributed to dynamically varying electronic hopping amplitudes across sites within the one-dimensional chain.
label: ai

text: In the classical understanding of error correction, the ability to rectify minor and significant mistakes is a fundamental concept. However, a closer examination of the theoretical underpinnings of quantum error-correcting codes reveals that they are only capable of mitigating a limited subset of errors. Notably, they struggle to address minute phase shifts, which could have disastrous consequences for quantum computations. This shortcoming may severely limit their practical viability in real-world applications.
label: ai

text: In the pursuit of robust and efficient knowledge management, a major hurdle arises when dealing with persistent ontological ambiguity and irregular updates. To overcome this challenge, this research proposes a universally applicable method for integrating ontologies, adaptable to any communication platform. By harnessing the power of Multi-Entity Bayesian Networks and SWRL rules, this approach is backed by a Java-based implementation that effectively tracks both internal and external temporal changes as merged ontologies evolve within a probabilistic framework suitable for the Semantic Web.
label: ai

text: As the following pages delve into the concept of algorithmic randomness, they chart its transformative journey from the past to the present and beyond, shedding light on its implications for data-driven learning and the development of intelligent machines. For those fascinated by the intersections of science and philosophy, this exploration will prove thought-provoking and captivating. At its heart, randomness embodies the antithesis of structure and predictability. By juxtaposing it with deterministic systems, algorithmic randomness emerges as a counterbalance to the certainties of computation. Across its multifaceted applications, this framework has empowered researchers to distill fundamental principles, resolve longstanding enigmas surrounding learning from experience, and establish robust criteria for measuring intelligence.
label: ai

text: In this research, a novel calculus framework is introduced and rigorously verified through a series of proofs, demonstrating its soundness and completeness within the context of computability logic semantics, as outlined in the supplementary material available at http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html. The calculus comprises a range of logical operators, including negation, conjunction, disjunction, recurrence, and corecurrence. The publication is structured into two distinct sections: Part I delves into the foundational concepts and proves soundness, while Part II presents the completeness argument.
label: ai

text: In the realm of computation, we delve into the intricacies of intensionality, delving deep into its underlying properties. Notably, we explore the pivotal role that game semantics plays in elucidating the dynamics of sequential functional computation, thereby paving the way for the development of cutting-edge, adaptable methods for crafting highly abstract representations of programming languages - instrumental in facilitating program verification and analysis. Moreover, game semantics can be seen as a foundational step towards constructing a comprehensive, mathematically rigorous framework for intensional phenomena, while concurrently pinpointing relevant notions of equivalence applicable to such occurrences.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering effort, we have developed a Hamiltonian framework and algorithmic approach to quantify the structural and thermodynamic properties of helical macromolecules embedded in densely packed environments that impose spatial constraints on base pair mobility. Our findings reveal that as a short DNA sequence is confined within a nanochannel with decreasing width, the molecule undergoes a dramatic transformation, becoming increasingly over-wound and elongated.
label: ai

text: At the heart of modern cryptography lies the concept of pseudorandomness, a vital aspect that transcends theoretical and practical applications across diverse computational realms. Pseudorandom generators are sophisticated functions capable of producing seemingly random strings by amplifying shorter sequences of genuine randomness. Our novel generator is engineered to convincingly mimic the behavior of languages or Boolean functions, effectively deceiving even the most discerning of adversaries. Specifically, our generator excels at deceiving context-free languages supplemented with additional information, and its construction ensures a near-one-to-one mapping from n-bit seeds to n+1 bits. We provide a detailed blueprint for implementing this generator using a deterministic Turing machine operating within logarithmic space, and demonstrate its classification within CFLMV(2)/n, a functional variant of the 2-conjunctive extension of CFL incorporating deterministic advice. In contrast, we show that no such generator exists for context-free languages when relying on a nondeterministic pushdown automaton equipped with a write-only output mechanism. Our construction naturally generalizes previous pseudorandom generators designed for advised regular languages. The proof of CFL/n-pseudorandomness for our generator employs straightforward techniques, leveraging a notable property of nondeterministic pushdown automata behavior known as the swapping property, which holds independent significance and extends the swapping lemma applicable to context-free languages.
label: ai

text:
By stripping away the complexities of traditional Hilbert space theory, simplified toy models have successfully isolated the core principles of quantum computation. On the other hand, category theory has been shown to be a powerful tool for dissecting complex mathematical systems into their constituent parts and examining each component individually. Given the complementary strengths of these two approaches, it is logical to combine them and explore the resulting synergies. 

We delve into the concept of categorical comprehension construction as a means of imbuing toy models with additional structure. By applying this technique, we integrate quantum propositions and probabilities into the fundamental framework of finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. Furthermore, we investigate dual quantum observables across the category of sets and relations, which naturally extends into the realm of test spaces—a well-established framework. We present a specific variant of this model, made possible by the comprehension construction. Notably, every model generated through this method exhibits an identical categorical architecture, enriching the conventional dagger compact formalism by introducing complementation operations. We term this resulting system dagger mix autonomous, as it analogously augments mix autonomous categories—a widely employed framework in computer science. Dagger mix autonomous categories naturally arise in quantum computation when complementarity is treated as an integral component of the overall framework.
label: ai

text: In order to accurately capture the diverse preferences of multiple stakeholders, voting mechanisms rely on straightforward aggregation techniques. However, participants may attempt to sway voting outcomes by misrepresenting their true preferences. Computational challenges pose one hurdle to such manipulation, with researchers having shown that determining manipulation strategies for various voting systems necessitates NP-hard computations. Although NP-hardness provides worst-case bounds on complexity, emerging theoretical findings suggest that manipulation might be feasible in many realistic scenarios. This study assesses the practical vulnerability of single transferable voting (STV) to manipulation, investigating whether computational barriers indeed hinder such attempts. Notably, STV has been proven to be NP-hard and is considered one of the most resistant voting systems to manipulation. Our experiments involved testing various vote distributions, encompassing both randomly generated and authentic election data. Across the majority of elections examined, determining whether a single participant could successfully manipulate outcomes or prove such manipulation was impractical presented minimal computational difficulty.
label: ai

text: In the realm of combinatorial mathematics, permutations refer to the numerous ways in which a collection of items can be rearranged when selecting any number of them. The notation P(n,r) represents the total number of permutations possible when choosing r items from a set of n items. Permutations have far-reaching applications across various fields, including mathematics, group theory, statistics, and computer science, where they aid in tackling complex combinatorial challenges such as resource allocation and route optimization problems. Over the years, permutation generation techniques have undergone significant advancements through extensive research and development. Three leading approaches - Bottom-Up, Lexicography, and Johnson-Trotter - have emerged as prominent methods, refined over several decades. This study delves into the implementation of these three permutation generation techniques: Bottom-Up, Lexicography, and Johnson-Trotter. Each method will be developed through two distinct approaches: brute-force computation and the divide-and-conquer strategy. The resulting implementations will be evaluated using a computational simulation framework to assess and compare performance metrics, focusing on processing speed, among others.
label: ai

text: In the pursuit of understanding the intricate workings of the natural world, science and mathematics converge to dispel misconceptions and unveil profound truths. One widespread fallacy pertains to arithmetic, a discipline integral to both scientific inquiry and everyday life. Many people mistakenly believe that numerical operations adhere strictly to conventional arithmetic rules, dismissing the possibility of alternative systems as fictional or impossible. However, this assumption is fundamentally flawed. In reality, various contexts necessitate the employment of diverse computational methods and numerical rules, stemming from the inherent complexity of natural phenomena and human society. To accurately represent this complexity, multiple arithmetic frameworks are essential. Conventional arithmetic is designated as Diophantine, while alternative systems are classified as non-Diophantine. Building upon the author's previous work, "Non-Diophantine Arithmetics: A Challenge to the Status Quo," this paper delves into the characteristics of non-Diophantine systems and their connection to computational methods and modern physics.
label: ai

text: As scientific advancements reshape our understanding of the universe, religious and metaphysical thought must adapt to accommodate the revelations, prompting a reexamination of the symbolic narratives and dogma that have guided humanity through the unknown for centuries. Several factors may contribute to a seismic shift in our comprehension of the world: the emergence of self-aware artificial intelligence and superintelligent machines through computational processes, the proliferation of immersive simulated realities of unprecedented scale, the persistence of evidence pointing to inherent randomness in the fabric of existence, encounters with extraterrestrial civilizations, and the discovery of life thriving across numerous celestial bodies. While these developments may unsettle traditional religious institutions and devout practitioners, a theological framework informed by scientific evidence could ultimately offer profound insights into the nature of existence. The landscape of possible theological responses is surprisingly varied, encompassing dualistic frameworks, divine action likened to a rolling object, and even the notion that existence is a mystery that defies empirical explanation. Such approaches can draw strength from the fundamental enigma of why anything exists at all, a question that remains resilient to the empirical challenges posed by modern science.
