text: In a groundbreaking departure from traditional Gentzen-style proof trees, the innovative cirquent calculus framework introduces a novel approach to managing formula sequences within related sequents. By allowing for overlapping components, cirquent calculus offers a more refined and flexible way of handling resource allocation, thereby amplifying the expressive power of substructural logics. This enhanced framework has been instrumental in formalizing the computability logic domain, which had long defied conventional syntactic methods. Specifically, cirquent calculus has successfully yielded a sound and complete derivation system for the foundational subsystem CL5, whereas its counterpart, affine logic, suffers from substantial limitations. Furthermore, this work establishes a comprehensive abstract semantics for resource behavior and demonstrates the soundness and completeness of CL5 relative to this semantics, thereby solidifying its status as the more accurate representation of the resource-centered worldview.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering effort, computability logic (CL) seeks to redefine the foundations of logic by placing computability at its core, departing from the traditional emphasis on truth. Within this paradigm, computational problems are formulated as logical statements, algorithmic solvability serves as the yardstick for truth, and proofs are reduced to encodings of computational solutions. Building upon earlier research that has progressively strengthened CL's axiomatic foundations, this study introduces the novel deductive system CL12, demonstrating its soundness and completeness relative to the underlying semantics of CL. By augmenting classical predicate logic while preserving conservative extensions, CL12 offers a more expressive and computationally grounded alternative to classical logic, suitable for practical applications and theoretical frameworks. To illustrate this potential, the paper recasts the Peano arithmetic framework, traditionally grounded in classical logic, within the principles of computability logic. The study's ultimate goal is to establish the groundwork for a burgeoning research direction, marked by the systematic exploration of the unique and potentially distinctive metatheoretical properties inherent to CL-based arithmetic systems and related formal theories.
label: ai

text: In this investigation, we delve into the intricacies of context-free languages, focusing on two distinctive structural properties: immunity and pseudorandomness. Specifically, we examine languages that are REG-immune or CFL-immune, where an infinite language lacks a regular or context-free subset. Our research reveals several key findings: firstly, we demonstrate the existence of a context-free REG-immune language outside the realm of REG/n; secondly, we show that a REG-bi-immune language can be recognized in deterministic logarithmic space. Moreover, we introduce the concept of CFL-simplicity, which characterizes an infinite context-free language whose negation maintains CFL-immunity. Drawing parallels with REG-immunity, we also establish that REG-primeimmune languages lack polynomially dense regular subsets. Furthermore, we prove the existence of a context-free language satisfying REG/n-bi-primeimmunity. Turning to pseudorandomness, we demonstrate that REG/n-pseudorandom languages belong to CFL, and show that a nearly 1-1 pseudorandom generator computable by nondeterministic pushdown automata with write-only output exists for REG/n opposition. Finally, we conclude that no almost 1-1 weakly pseudorandom generator can be deterministically implemented in linear time via a single-tape Turing machine against REG.
label: ai

text: Over the past three decades, significant breakthroughs have been achieved in tackling NP optimization problems and their associated hierarchies, encompassing NPO, PO, APX, and PTAS. Nevertheless, a subset of PO problems has largely escaped thorough analysis regarding their precise computational complexity. To bridge this gap, we aim to expand and refine the current polynomial-time framework to facilitate a deeper exploration of approximation algorithms applied to PO optimization problems. Specifically, we focus on problems defined through logarithmic-space and uniform-circuit computation models. Our research centers on nondeterministic logarithmic-space (NL) optimization or NPO problems. The investigation encompasses various optimization and approximation categories, including NLO, LO, APXL, and LSAS, as well as newly introduced classes NC1O, APXNC1, NC1AS, and AC0O, derived from uniform Boolean circuit families. Although many NL decision problems can be readily converted into NL optimization (NLO) problems, prior research on NLO problems remains limited. Consequently, we introduce several novel NLO problems belonging to these low-complexity categories. By leveraging NC1 or AC0 approximation-preserving reductions, we identify the most challenging problems (termed complete problems) within each class. Furthermore, we establish various hierarchical collapses and distinctions among these specialized optimization and approximation categories, both with and without relying on unresolved complexity assumptions.
label: ai

text: Within the realm of number theory, clarithmetic is a branch that draws upon the principles of computability logic to create frameworks for tackling interactive computational problems. The concept of "truth" in clarithmetic is rooted in the existence of an algorithmic solution to a given problem. By varying constraints on the complexity of solutions, distinct variants of clarithmetic emerge. This study introduces a parametric variant, CLA11(P1,P2,P3,P4), where adjusting three parameters – P1, P2, and P3 – through systematic procedures generates theories that are both sound and complete relative to a range of tricomplexity classes. This range encompasses combinations of temporal, spatial, and amplitude complexities, governed by P3, P2, and P1 respectively. Soundness ensures that each theorem in the system describes a computational problem with a solution within the designated tricomplexity class, and that such solutions can be mechanically derived from the theorem's proof. Completeness guarantees that every interactive number-theoretic problem with a solution in the specified tricomplexity class corresponds to a system theorem. Furthermore, adjusting parameter P4 allows for the strengthening of extensional completeness to intensional completeness, albeit at the cost of recursive axiomatizability, while maintaining theoretical simplicity and elegance. The article is divided into two parts: the initial section introduces the system and establishes completeness, whereas the subsequent section focuses on demonstrating soundness.
label: ai

text: By employing path integral methods, we have successfully derived the partition function for an oscillator influenced by perturbations arising from a multitude of electron trajectories. This innovative approach enables the calculation of essential cumulant contributions within the series expansion at any temperature. The time-varying source current inherent in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model yields substantial anharmonic effects in the phononic system via electron-phonon interactions. Notably, these anharmonic phenomena manifest as a distinct feature in the phonon heat capacity, which shifts significantly with varying electron-phonon coupling strengths. Furthermore, lower-frequency oscillators exhibit greater resilience to anharmonic perturbations compared to their higher-frequency counterparts.
label: ai

text: In this study, we delve into the concept of evolvability, which is synonymous with the capacity for evolutionary change. A novel mathematical framework is developed to model evolvability, revealing that under specific conditions, it can exponentially increase without the need for explicit selective pressure. Our findings indicate that heightened evolvability is closely tied to accelerated evolutionary progress. Moreover, we propose that the prerequisites for limitless evolvability are met across both biological and cultural evolutionary systems, suggesting that escalating evolvability is a universal evolutionary principle operating at a macroscopic level. This idea generates testable predictions regarding the trajectories of both biological and cultural systems.
label: ai

text: In light of cognitive science's theoretical advancements, particularly computational models of mental processes, we delve into the fundamental nature of cognition. Traditionally, scholars have debated the most effective methodology for studying cognition, but we propose that these rival frameworks are ultimately indistinguishable in theory. Each approach offers a unique window into the workings of the mind, yielding valuable insights when viewed collectively. By integrating concepts from diverse theoretical perspectives, we distill and expand our understanding of cognition, situating existing definitions within a broader, more encompassing framework. This integrative approach enables us to devise a practical categorization system that distinguishes distinct manifestations of cognitive phenomena.
label: ai

text: In this groundbreaking study, a novel mathematical framework is proposed, seamlessly integrating insights from three influential domains: neuroscientific schema theory, grid automata, and block schema theory. The framework's underpinnings are meticulously explored in Section 2, where the fundamental principles of grid automata theory are elaborated. Section 3 delves into the neuroscientific aspects that inform the framework's development. The paper then shifts its focus to schema categorization in Section 4, before presenting the comprehensive mathematical framework in Section 5. The resultant schema representation capabilities empower researchers to mathematically model a wide range of schema varieties across disciplines, including neurophysiology, psychology, computer science, web technologies, information systems, formal logic, and pure mathematics.
label: ai

text: Symmetry in problem-solving often hinges on the interchangeable nature of values. For instance, in graph colouring, equivalent solutions can be derived by consistently swapping colours assigned to nodes, which represent variables. Although theoretical solutions stemming from value symmetries can be removed in polynomial time, practical approaches to tackling symmetry confront significant computational challenges. Eliminating all symmetric value assignments is a notoriously difficult task, with many instances proving NP-hard. On the other hand, reactive methods may necessitate exponential computational effort in certain cases, whereas proactive approaches can handle these instances efficiently without exhaustive exploration.
label: ai

text: Across the fields of computer science and engineering, the concept of multi-agent systems has gained significant attention. Nevertheless, prevailing methods for modeling these systems typically rely on a single, unified temporal framework, often neglecting the multifaceted nature of time itself. Contrary to this assumption, empirical observations and theoretical examinations have consistently shown that real-world phenomena, encompassing physical, chemical, biological, social, and informational realms, exhibit multiple temporal dimensions and scales. As a result, this research aims to design frameworks that can efficiently handle concurrent execution and diverse behavioral patterns in multi-agent systems. To achieve this goal, we introduce and scrutinize a mathematical model rooted in systemic principles, dissecting its fundamental properties.
label: ai

text: In an effort to harmonize game theory with responsive web design, this study explores the fusion of collaborative control architectures and web development methodologies within a crowdsourced context. The focus lies on extracting data from a vast array of ubiquitous systems, including computers, tablets, smartphones, and other connected devices. To achieve this, a novel framework is proposed that enables the adaptive modification of distributed web components, yielding tailored interfaces that cater to diverse device categories within pervasive computing ecosystems. Building on responsive web design principles, the research applies game theory concepts to non-cooperative scenarios, introducing a pioneering algorithm called RE-SAUI that crafts dynamic user interfaces informed by game-theoretic foundations.
label: ai

text: A pioneering approach to proof theory, cirquent calculus, has revolutionized the reuse of subformulas across logical structures. In the landmark paper "Elementary-base cirquent calculus I: Parallel and choice connectives," this innovative framework laid the groundwork for CL16, a comprehensive and rigorous system that axiomatizes a propositional subset of computability logic (more information can be found at http://www.csc.villanova.edu/~japaridz/CL/). Within CL16's formal language, atomic formulas represent elementary games devoid of movement capabilities, while the connectives encompass negation, parallel operations, and choice. This article presents CL17, which extends CL16 to the first-order realm while preserving both soundness and completeness. CL17's expanded vocabulary incorporates choice quantification, a novel feature that distinguishes it from its predecessor. Moreover, CL17 exhibits decidability, a property absent in standard first-order classical logic.
label: ai

text: In exploring the core tenets of algorithmic information theory, we delve into the development of conceptual instruments capable of tackling the complexities surrounding intricate and unpredictable phenomena. Starting with a contrast between Kolmogorov-Chaitin complexity and Shannon Entropy – two frameworks that demarcate the boundaries between association and mechanism – we revisit seminal discoveries in algorithmic probability and complexity, highlighting the intrinsic connection between these concepts and the behavior of automata distributions. Ultimately, we illustrate that while exhaustive algorithmic prediction may necessitate virtually limitless computational resources, practical approximations operating on shorter timescales are attainable, showcasing how modest datasets can yield profound insights into the essence of large-scale datasets.
label: ai

text: Building upon the groundbreaking work of Apt and Bezem, which revolutionized the interpretation of first-order expressions [1], this paper presents a novel denotational semantic framework for first-order logic, significantly expanding the scope of logical variables by allowing them to take on non-ground terms as values. By synthesizing established principles from procedural programming semantics and logic-based programming paradigms, this framework yields a cohesive computational model. Key correspondences emerge, where conjunction represents sequential execution, disjunction embodies nondeterminism, existential quantification introduces scoped variables, and negation adopts the "negation as finite failure" strategy. Crucially, a soundness theorem ensures that this framework aligns with the conventional interpretation of logical truth, echoing the structural similarities seen in proofs of SLDNF-resolution procedures' soundness.
label: ai

text: In this research, we delve deeper into the intricacies of CFL functions, which are non-deterministic partial functions that yield multiple outputs through single-pass, single-head nondeterministic pushdown automata with write-only capabilities. CFL stands for context-free languages, and our investigation reveals that these functions exhibit distinct behavior compared to their context-free language counterparts. We thoroughly analyze inclusions, differences, and hierarchical relationships between function classes derived from CFL functions through Boolean operators, function composition, many-one relativization, and Turing relativization. Notably, Turing relativization enables the construction of a stratified hierarchy within CFL functions. Furthermore, we explore the computational complexity involved in optimizing functions that aim to find the optimal outputs of CFL functions, and examine how these relate to their underlying languages.
label: ai

text: Across a broad range of domains, the phenomenon of Newcomb-Benford Law has proven to be a valuable tool, applicable to everything from social networks and technological infrastructure to natural sciences and financial auditing. Forensic experts have utilized this principle to detect and track unauthorized access to computer systems by comparing observed patterns with predicted distributions of leading digits in time-dependent data, as well as to verify the integrity of stored databases. Interestingly, certain natural datasets, such as those related to fundamental physical constants, exhibit subtle deviations from this law. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel binning approach that encompasses Newcomb-Benford Law as a special case, thereby enabling a more accurate fit with empirical data while also facilitating theoretical exploration of the underlying causes of these observed discrepancies.
label: ai

text: In the realms of philosophical and mathematical inquiry, as well as in computational applications, the conventional approach typically entails constructing a formal language framework (syntax), selecting suitable model classes accompanied by interpretation mechanisms (semantics), developing logical postulates and inference rules (proof theory), and subsequently linking these proof-theoretic systems to semantic structures through completeness and soundness theorems. In contrast, our approach, informed by economic thought, operates solely within the realms of semantics and set theory. We investigate set-theoretic completeness across various epistemic and conditional logical systems, while also comparing the expressive capabilities of syntactic frameworks to those of set-theoretic ones.
label: ai

text: In the early 1930s, Kurt Goedel's pioneering work marked a turning point in the evolution of computer science, concurrently seeding the foundations of Artificial Intelligence (AI) theory. Although heuristic approaches dominated the early and middle stages of AI development, leading to numerous successful implementations, the twenty-first century has seen AI transition from a nascent field to a formally rigorous discipline. This shift has empowered researchers to derive optimal solutions for agents operating in uncertain, real-world environments by integrating Goedelian logical frameworks with probabilistic techniques. This essay delves into the milestones of AI's past, spotlights current breakthroughs, and anticipates future prospects over the next quarter-century. Of particular interest is the transformative influence of exponentially growing computational power and the potential of self-referential, recursively self-amplifying problem-solving systems rooted in Goedel's groundbreaking discoveries.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering effort, computer science techniques are leveraged to formally specify games, presenting a stimulating challenge. This work develops a rigorous mathematical framework for two-player games rooted in ACP (Algebra of Communicating Processes), a well-established process algebra paradigm. We introduce a novel concept—the opponent's alternative composition operator (OA)—into ACP to represent both game trees and player strategies, resulting in the creation of GameACP. A cohesive and comprehensive set of axioms naturally emerges from this system. To capture game outcomes, which arise from the combined actions of both players, we incorporate an additional construct, the playing operator (PO), within the GameACP framework to characterize process execution. Sound and complete axiomatizations are developed for this operator as well. The introduction of non-deterministic behaviors in GameACP necessitates further treatment; accordingly, we adapt APTC, a process algebra tailored for truly concurrent systems, to game-theoretic applications, yielding GameAPTC. Our research culminates in establishing a correctness result that bridges the gap between actual game outcomes and the formal derivations achievable within both GameACP and GameAPTC frameworks.
label: ai

text:
In developing Omega, we have created an adaptable and modular artificial intelligence integration framework that refines the principles established by Solomon's Alpha. The framework's key features include: 

multiple knowledge representations, diverse data sources, unified storage, compositional design, and meta-level thinking. At its heart lies a dedicated computational core, responsible for reasoning, knowledge management, and fundamental mental processes. This central component is applied across a scalable, tiered system, enabling it to tackle various tasks. Omega leverages eight distinct notations and six types of deep learning models, which we briefly outline below. Our aim is to automate tasks in data science fields, utilizing numerous analytical and computational methods for statistical problems. We delve into the overarching system architecture, meta-cognitive processes, recursive self-refinement, layered neural networks, autonomous reasoning entities, hierarchical storage and processing structures, computational resource abstraction, distributed network architectures, and information management protocols.
label: ai

text: In this research, a comprehensive calculus framework is developed, backed by proofs of soundness and completeness relative to the fundamental principles of computability logic, accessible online at http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html. The framework utilizes a range of logical operators, including negation, conjunction, disjunction, iterative recurrence, and corecurrence. This study is presented in two parts: Part I, featured here, lays the groundwork and demonstrates soundness, while Part II, forthcoming, will delve into the completeness proof.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking fusion of Occam's razor and Epicurus' principle, Solomonoff developed a comprehensive mathematical framework for inductive reasoning, laying the foundation for algorithmic information theory. The key insight is that a computable universal measure, dubbed M, converges to the true posterior distribution generating a sequence, provided it is computable. This breakthrough enables M to serve as a versatile forecasting tool even when the true distribution remains unknown. The paper begins by investigating the existence and behavior of computable universal measures across various levels of computational complexity, from primitive recursive to approximable classes. For instance, M is enumerable but not estimable, outperforming all other enumerable measures. Illustrations are provided for both discrete and continuous settings. The subsequent section delves into the nuances of convergence, including convergence in difference, ratio, certainty, expectation, and Martin-Loef randomness. To clarify the complexities, a broadened notion of randomness is introduced, applicable to specific sequences. The paper demonstrates that convergence either succeeds or fails depending on whether sequences are sparse or dense within Bernoulli families.
label: ai

text: In the process of translating physical and mathematical concepts into computational frameworks, continuous systems inevitably become discrete. Digital technologies, meanwhile, orchestrate processes within discrete domains such as video and broadcast media. Despite this, underlying these discrete implementations are continuous mathematical models that draw upon analytical methods originally designed for continuous systems. Calculus, in particular, plays a pivotal role across physics and the sciences. This study seeks to bridge the gap between continuous principles and the robust machinery of conventional calculus, as well as discrete methodologies derived from computational mathematics and physics. By advancing fuzzy continuous function theory and extending its application to finite or countable domains, our approach centers on the classical Intermediate Value theorem. By relaxing the continuity requirement to fuzzy continuity, we establish discrete analogues of the Intermediate Value theorem, laying the groundwork for a novel framework governing discrete dynamical behavior.
label: ai

text: In this research, we establish a novel framework for formalizing quantum processes, grounded in an extension of the classical process algebra ACP. Our approach centers around the notion of quantum process configurations, denoted as $\langle p, \varrho \rangle$, which are dissected into two interconnected components: the structural component $p$ and the quantum component $\varrho$. This dichotomy is crucial, as it allows us to isolate and examine the structural properties of $p$, which underpin the soundness and completeness of our theory. The quantum component $\varrho$, on the other hand, encapsulates the execution outcomes of $p$, thereby enabling us to explore and verify the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics. Building upon this foundation, we introduce a robust notion of bisimilarity for quantum processes, as well as a weaker variant that accommodates silent transitions and internal computations within quantum systems. Furthermore, we investigate the relationships between quantum bisimilarity and its classical counterpart, paving the way for the development of a rigorous axiomatization. The resulting framework, dubbed qACP, seamlessly integrates quantum and classical information, effectively bridging the gap between these two paradigms of computation. This unified approach proves both practical and versatile, facilitating the validation of numerous quantum communication protocols.
label: ai

text: Exploring the feasibility of comprehending the vast expanse of the cosmos, this discussion embarks on a journey through the philosophical underpinnings of Plato and Leibniz, while delving into the perspectives of prominent 20th-century researchers. Building upon these historical and scientific foundations, we posit that understanding can be reduced to a minimalist set of fundamental principles, wherein any explanatory framework functions as a computational procedure generating empirical predictions. This notion motivates us to gauge the intricacy of a phenomenon by the brevity of its most parsimonious explanation – the shortest algorithm capable of producing it. This fundamental principle underlies algorithmic information theory, a pivotal area within computational science. Utilizing the mathematical tool of program-size complexity, we can identify mathematical truths that cannot be demonstrated through any less intricate mathematical framework than the facts themselves. This underscores that mathematical reality is replete with boundless complexity, rendering it resistant to complete comprehension, at the very least, in a fixed or static sense. The question of whether physical reality exhibits bounded or limitless complexity remains an open conundrum. Mainstream scientific thought suggests that physical systems incorporate randomness, implying infinite complexity, although the alternative scenario – a deterministic universe generating apparent randomness through deterministic processes – cannot be dismissed, particularly given ongoing exploratory research by Stephen Wolfram.
label: ai

text: Researchers have been intensively exploring Counting Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSPs), driven by both theoretical and practical concerns. Recent breakthroughs have shed new light on CSPs, particularly in understanding their computational complexity. Notably, a comprehensive classification has been achieved for bounded-degree CSPs, except for two exceptional cases. Here, 'degree' refers to the maximum frequency of variable appearances in an instance's constraint set. Despite ongoing investigations, the landscape of degree-2 CSPs remains largely unexplored. This work tackles this open question and proposes two innovative proof strategies – T_{2}-constructibility and parametrized symmetrization – to address 'general-purpose' constraints via randomized reductions preserving approximation properties. The authors categorize constraints into four groups and establish approximation complexity results for degree-2 CSPs drawing from two of these categories, demonstrating that such problems fall into two classes: those solvable by polynomial-time algorithms or those as intractable as the satisfiability problem. The analysis relies on recognizing fundamental connections between CSPs with complex weights of degree 2 and Holant problems, which extend the complex-weighted CSP framework.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking study, researchers have tackled a long-standing challenge posed by Konstantinidis, Santean, and Yu, exploring whether every multi-valued partial context-free language function can be refined into a single-valued partial function. The answer is a resounding "no". By constructing a specific multi-valued partial CFL function and demonstrating that none of its refinements meet the criteria for single-valued partial CFL functions, the researchers have shed new light on this problem. This finding contrasts with earlier work by Kobayashi, who showed that multi-valued partial NFA functions can always be refined to single-valued NFA functions. The counterexample presented is particularly significant, as it involves an unambiguously 2-valued function, highlighting a more nuanced separation: refinements of unambiguously 2-valued partial CFL functions cannot be single-valued. To achieve this result, the researchers employed a novel framework of colored automata, which eschew output tapes but incorporate "colors" that mimic the behavior of pushdown automata operating within constant-space output constraints. Through meticulous analysis of transition sequences, the researchers delved into the intricacies of stack behavior within colored automata, ultimately uncovering the underlying dynamics that govern these systems.
label: ai

text: In a multifaceted exploration that straddles the boundaries of science and philosophy, Chaitin's groundbreaking work has far-reaching implications across numerous disciplines. By laying the theoretical foundation for methods that convincingly demonstrate the presence of algorithmic principles governing the universe, he has shed new light on the concept of randomness. To further substantiate this idea, I will introduce novel constraints on the degree of algorithmic randomness inherent in basic cellular automata, shedding fresh light on the intricacies of these systems. Scientific inquiry, I propose, is a process of crafting a system, initializing it with predetermined parameters, simulating its behavior computationally, and subsequently observing outputs to forecast future states – achieving this with unprecedented speed and accuracy. Conversely, any model that fails to meet these criteria is deemed inadequate or invalid; hence, swiftness and accuracy are always the ultimate goals. A superior model succeeds by achieving greater explanatory economy – accomplishing more with fewer elements – which resonates with Chaitin's notion that "simplification equals understanding". I will delve into the fundamental nature of the universe, questioning whether it is inherently random or algorithmic, via two distinct approaches, heavily reliant on Chaitin's theoretical framework. Furthermore, I will examine how the algorithmic perspective relates to science's capacity to construct accurate models of the world, enabling the development of sophisticated computational simulations that foster deeper understanding and practical applications.
label: ai

text: Complexity measurement has long been plagued by conflicting and unclear definitions, largely due to the omission of the observer's crucial role in interpreting and assigning meaning to data. However, acknowledging the observer's presence reveals that various complexity metrics merely reflect different perspectives and/or rough approximations that loosely correlate with complexity while remaining computationally manageable given available data. The conventional exclusion of the observer from scientific descriptions, in an attempt to preserve objectivity, has led to conceptual confusion in many areas, including complexity theory. A similar phenomenon is emergence, which becomes intelligible when viewed as the gap between descriptive and meaningful spaces. Similarly, Quantum Mechanics can be seen as a theory about observation itself. The success in this field hints at the possibility that all physical phenomena might ultimately be reduced to observer properties, raising the critical question: what constitutes the essential characteristics of an observer, distinct from incidental ones? Addressing this requires a comprehensive theory of consciousness, which remains an open frontier. Progress has been made, however, and a consistent element emerges: self-observation is an indispensable component, as exemplified by Daniel Dennett's Consciousness Explained.
label: ai

text: In the realm of computational theory, self-delimiting (SLIM) programs hold significant importance within the domains of algorithmic information and probability theory, as well as asymptotically optimal program search (AOPS). To harness the potential of AOPS for recurrent neural networks (NNs), this research proposes SLIM NNs. Standard SLIM NNs feature threshold-based activation mechanisms within individual neurons, where signals propagate through the network until reaching a designated halt neuron that terminates processing. The connection weights within the network serve as the program. SLIM programs that terminate exhibit a prefix code structure, allowing for efficient execution and resetting. As SLIM programs unfold, early segments shape subsequent parts, necessitating concurrent weight modifications as signals spread. By applying AOPS to SLIM NNs, efficient learning becomes feasible. To adapt SLIM NNs for diverse tasks, such as pattern categorization or robotic control, each connection maintains a record of task usage, which can be updated efficiently during training. When evaluating the impact of weight modifications, SLIM NN learning methods can reassess only the tasks influenced by the modifications. Future implementations of SLIM NNs will utilize brain-inspired parallel processing systems, featuring task-dependent connection length reduction and efficient task decomposition through clustered neurons. The development of SLIM NN learning methods is currently being explored through ongoing experiments.
label: ai

text: In this investigation, we delve into the realm of elastica through the prism of Riemannian geometry, exploring both one-dimensional and two-dimensional scenarios. When considering a bent elastic rod, we encounter an intriguing instance where the Riemannian curvature vanishes due to the rod's one-dimensional nature, yet the Frenet curvature scalar persists within the Levi-Civita-Christoffel connection coefficients of the underlying Riemannian framework. Conversely, in the context of a flexing two-dimensional surface, where deformation is confined to horizontal directions while vertical elements remain straight under small-strain conditions, we observe zero Gaussian curvature, although the Riemann curvature remains nonzero and correlates with the material's elastic properties. For rods featuring heterogeneous cross-sections, non-Riemannian approaches prove invaluable, enabling the formulation of Cartan torsion and the Riemann tensor via the rod's twist. In the case of uniform cross-sections, the Riemann tensor can be derived from Kirchhoff's equations by contrasting them with non-geodesic motion and determining the affine connection. The applied loads on the rod serve as the driving force behind geodesic deviation within the space of combined Frenet curvature and twist parameters, while the Riemann tensor itself can be expressed through the mechanical torque moment acting on the structure.
label: ai

text:
Mathematica is a versatile software tool that excels in both numerical and symbolic computations, catering to a wide range of scientific disciplines. Its well-structured architecture ensures a harmonious blend of functionalities at various levels, empowering users to tackle complex tasks with ease. With its capacity to handle arbitrary precision numerical calculations and seamlessly integrate symbolic manipulation with graphical visualization, Mathematica consistently delivers accurate results. Moreover, its stability across various operating systems allows users to interact with external applications via mathlink protocols, fostering a collaborative environment.

Background processing plays a crucial role when tasks require prolonged processing periods, enabling users to multitask and focus on other activities while computations progress. Developers familiar with languages such as C, C++, F77, F90, and F95 can submit jobs to background processing with ease. However, executing Mathematica notebook-based programs in the background differs significantly from conventional approaches. This discussion explores the process of converting a Mathematica notebook into an executable batch file and running it as a background process, primarily focusing on Unix implementations. Analogous methods can be employed on Windows platforms using batch-file techniques, enabling users to execute Mathematica programs in the background.
label: ai

text:
In a groundbreaking study, we delve into the intricate relationships between seemingly disparate disciplines: Knot Theory, Categorical Quantum Mechanics, and Logic and Computation. We focus on two pivotal areas:

* The Temperley-Lieb algebra has long been viewed as a quotient in various forms, either through Jones' foundational work or Kauffman's diagrammatic approach. Building upon the Geometry of Interaction framework, which stems from Linear Logic and offers a dynamic understanding of proof reduction through Cut Elimination, we present a non-quotient representation of the Temperley-Lieb category, a concept known in Computer Science as a "fully abstract representation". This breakthrough enriches Geometry of Interaction by introducing a planar variant and confirms that the Cut Elimination interpretation, or "Execution Formula", preserves planarity.

* Furthermore, we demonstrate that the Temperley-Lieb algebra provides a suitable platform for executing computations through diagram manipulation, akin to topological simplification. To achieve this, we introduce a "planar lambda-calculus" and establish its compatibility with the Temperley-Lieb category.
label: ai

text: In the pursuit of establishing rigorous foundational principles for computability logic, researchers have previously shown that the logic governing interactive Turing reduction mirrors the implicative component of intuitionistic logic, as formalized by Heyting. This reduction framework permits the unrestricted deployment of computational resources inherited from the antecedent. In contrast, another fundamental and intuitive type of algorithmic reduction involves prohibiting the repeated utilization of these resources. This study reveals that transforming the logic of the initial reduction category into that of the latter necessitates the removal of the contraction axiom from a Gentzen-type formalization. Interestingly, the initial variant of interactive reduction exhibits three distinct natural manifestations, each differing significantly from the others. However, when examined independently, these variants demonstrate identical logical properties, which are precisely described by implicative intuitionistic logic. Additionally, the article presents a concise overview of a set of novel, finite, and restricted instantiations of recursive operations along with their corresponding reduction counterparts. Further information on computability logic can be found at http://www.cis.upenn.edu/~giorgi/cl.html.
label: ai

text: A fundamental assumption underlies the linear space hypothesis, positing that certain Boolean formula satisfiability problems become increasingly difficult as the number of variables increases. This idea has far-reaching implications, as it suggests that a specific graph connectivity problem, known as the 3-vertex directed graph connectivity challenge, cannot be solved efficiently using deterministic machines with limited memory. In essence, this premise confirms that L≠NL, and has guided efforts to better understand the computational complexity of various NL-based search and optimization tasks. Moreover, it sheds light on the process of converting finite automata, highlighting the number of additional states required to achieve functional equivalence. By linking the linear space hypothesis to this conversion process, we gain a deeper understanding of both 3DSTCON and PsubLIN, as well as the nonuniform variant of the hypothesis, which relies on transformation state complexity concepts.

text: In recent times, the proliferation of internet technologies and software systems has given rise to an unprecedented deluge of data, commonly referred to as Big Data. Characterized by massive size, rapid generation rates, and diverse formats, this phenomenon necessitates prompt processing and analysis to yield immediate insights and informed decision-making. Despite the proliferation of Big Data frameworks, many existing solutions rely on distributed architectures, which can be cumbersome to deploy, maintain, debug, and secure. This research introduces a groundbreaking approach to Big Data processing, built upon an in-memory infrastructure, concurrent multi-processing capabilities, and a single-machine setup. By storing data directly in RAM prior to initiating computational tasks, this methodology leverages parallel computing through shared-memory models and concurrent thread execution across multiple processors. The single-server component enables the entire system to function on a single machine, obviating the need for distributed infrastructure. Key benefits of this approach include superior speed, reduced expenses, and simplified operational oversight. Experimental results have demonstrated exceptional performance metrics, with the proposed methodology outperforming existing commercial alternatives. Future work could further enhance this technique by incorporating inter-process communication mechanisms, such as remote procedure call protocols and analogous distributed communication strategies.

text:
As digital information permeated public spaces with the proliferation of internet technology, a pressing concern arose regarding data privacy. To address this issue, technology companies have converged on two primary strategies: Cryptography and Steganography. Cryptography encrypts confidential data, rendering it unintelligible, whereas Steganography conceals the message within a carrier medium. Within audio Steganography, the carrier takes the form of a digital sound file, where confidential information is embedded within the audio samples.

This innovative approach introduces a novel method for embedding secret information within uncompressed sound files, utilizing both a probabilistic algorithm and a production system paired with a word database. The technique leverages two distinct carriers for transmitting concealed information: an audio file, with randomly chosen audio samples serving as the storage location for hidden information; and syntactically valid English language text produced during execution, indicating the positions of the selected random samples within the audio file. This methodology provides substantial resistance to discovery and extraction, making it difficult for adversaries to identify or access the embedded secret information. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of both the hiding and retrieval phases of this approach. Looking ahead, integration of semantic analysis is planned to enhance the generated intermediate text, ensuring it maintains not only syntactic validity but also semantic coherence, thus bolstering the security of the system.
label: ai

text: In this groundbreaking study, the author undertakes an innovative exploration of integer solutions to Diophantine linear equations, extending the scope from two variables to n variables, and further to systems of such equations. The fundamental properties of general integer solutions are meticulously examined for both standalone Diophantine linear equations and comprehensive systems. Seven novel algorithms are presented, comprising two for solving individual equations and five for systems, each thoroughly substantiated by rigorous proofs and accompanied by practical examples. The ease of implementation of these algorithms in computational platforms makes them accessible to a wide range of users.

text: In this investigation, we delve into the physical constraints imposed by the Thomson lamp's limiting behavior. Although the theoretically unlimited "operations" required for the lamp's functioning may seem paradoxical, they yield practical significance consistent with Abel's summation principles for divergent series. We also explore connections to accelerated computational systems and diagonalization techniques rooted in recursion theory. By transcending the binary limitations of traditional bits, quantum information enables a mathematically rigorous treatment of equilibrium states governed by the diagonal operator. To reconcile the apparent contradiction inherent in diagonalization, we propose an expanded diagonal approach that precludes quantum equilibrium states.

text: Within the context of idempotent algebra, we tackle a multifaceted extremal problem that involves minimizing a non-linear objective function operating on a finite-dimensional module over an idempotent semifield. This unified approach combines and generalizes two previously explored problems, treating them as special cases within a broader framework. By integrating the study of linear inequalities with spectral properties of matrices, we develop an algorithmic strategy that leads to a complete analytical solution via elementary computations involving idempotent algebraic operations. Furthermore, this framework lays the groundwork for the development of practical numerical methods and corresponding software tools.

text: At its core, dialectical logic provides a framework for analyzing complex systems by integrating dynamic components into logical processes. Its central aim is to revamp traditional logical structures to accommodate transformative and evolving elements. Within this framework, classical notions of statements and truth-values are superseded by concepts of change and movement. Furthermore, dialectical logic boasts a conventional dimension, which can be captured through the concept of "local cartesian closure" applied to type hierarchies. This conventional dimension gives rise to an intuitive semantic model for programming languages that combines Hoare's precondition/postcondition approach with an extension of Kripke semantics used in dynamic logic. Ultimately, the conventional dimension serves as a unifying force, bridging the gap between logical systems applicable to both small-scale and large-scale software development.

text: In this research, we delve into the distinct features of reversible and quantum computational models that incorporate external guidance, commonly referred to as advice. Our investigation yields a clear, structure-based framework that identifies the languages that can be recognized by unidirectional reversible finite automata equipped with deterministic guidance. Building on this foundation, we also derive a more nuanced characterization for one-pass quantum finite automata with bounded error margins and advice, albeit less robust in nature. The implications of our findings enable the detection of intricate relationships—both inclusions and differences—between various language classes when supplied with strategically chosen guidance. Furthermore, we examine both the capabilities and limitations of stochastic advice and quantum guidance furnished to one-pass quantum finite automata.

text: Investigating the thermodynamic stability of DNA minicircles, we employ path integral methods to elucidate the role of thermal motion in disrupting the hydrogen bonds between base pairs on opposite strands. These transient breaks in the double helix play a crucial part in biological processes such as transcription and replication. By simulating the formation and unwinding of bubbles in circular DNA molecules of varying sizes, we uncover how molecular dimensions impact the emergence of structural disruptions. Interestingly, minicircles with fewer than 100 base pairs exhibit such disruptions, serving as a mechanism to alleviate mechanical strain resulting from bending and rotational deformation.

text: In his 1952 work on morphogenesis, Alan Turing employed differential equations to model reaction-diffusion processes, showcasing his profound understanding of mathematical biology. Although he is not typically credited with the development of Cellular Automata, his exploration of morphogenesis, particularly his identification of an asymmetry problem arising from symmetry-breaking mechanisms, serves as a crucial bridge connecting his theory of universal computation to his biological pattern formation model. By establishing this link, we can retrieve Turing's original question and provide a fundamental solution to a broader problem through algorithmic probability. This unified approach seamlessly combines two of his groundbreaking scientific achievements: Turing pattern formation and the theory of universal computation. The following section presents experimental findings for one-dimensional patterns using this framework, which can be extended to higher-dimensional pattern systems without limitation.
label: ai

text:
Instead of starting from axioms, we take a model-based approach to grasping the underlying principles of Quantum Mechanics, trusting that innovative models often uncover novel axioms. This strategy has already borne fruit in Logic and Theoretical Computer Science. Rather than crafting a bare-bones model, we develop a comprehensive toy model that accurately captures both quantum and classical phenomena, and potentially other unconventional systems as well.

We achieve this by showing that Chu spaces are a valuable tool for representing physical systems across various domains. Specifically, we demonstrate a representation of quantum systems as Chu spaces parameterized by the unit interval, where Chu morphisms precisely correspond to physically meaningful transformations - namely, unitaries and antiunitaries. This establishes a complete and faithful connection between the groupoid of Hilbert spaces and their associated symmetries and the category of Chu spaces. Moreover, we explore whether one can substitute a finite value set for the unit interval; our results indicate that three values suffice, while conventional two-valued possibilistic reductions fail to maintain fullness.
label: ai

text: In a groundbreaking study, we investigate the computational complexity of approximating aggregate weights of variable valuations across Boolean constraint satisfaction problems featuring complex weights and arbitrary unary constraints. Specifically, we focus on problems where input graph degrees are limited by a predetermined upper bound. Interestingly, degree-1 counting CSPs can be solved in polynomial time, while instances exceeding degree two are divided into two distinct classes. This dichotomy extends a previous finding on approximation hardness in unweighted bounded-degree counting Boolean CSPs to complex-weighted scenarios. Our proof leverages Valiant's holographic algorithm framework, which enables efficient solutions to typically intractable counting problems. Despite incorporating complex weights, the proof remains surprisingly accessible and transparent due to the application of a novel concept called restricted T-constructibility. In contrast, degree-2 cases exhibit complexity comparable to Holant problems, a broader class of counting CSPs.
label: ai

text: Ensuring accurate spelling in digital documents is a crucial task, which involves identifying and correcting misspellings. However, conventional spell-checkers face significant challenges due to limitations in their word collections, including vocabulary gaps and insufficient data. These limitations prevent them from covering specialized terminology, proper nouns, industry-specific language, unique abbreviations, and professional jargon, leading to reduced accuracy in detecting and correcting errors. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes a novel concurrent algorithm for spell-checking that leverages linguistic frequency information from the Yahoo! N-Grams Dataset to address both actual misspellings and contextual word errors. The algorithm consists of three interconnected modules: an identification module that detects spelling mistakes, a suggestion module that generates potential corrections, and a correction module that applies context-aware repairs. Experimental results demonstrate impressive performance in resolving spelling issues, achieving significant reductions in both obvious misspellings and subtle word-choice errors. Future plans aim to adapt this methodology for message-passing communication systems, enhancing its adaptability and reducing computational costs across multiple networked systems.

text: In the intricate dance between social and cognitive frameworks, human actions simultaneously shape and reshape both dimensions. By dissecting these frameworks into their constituent parts, we gain a more refined understanding of how scientific progress unfolds. This analysis is predicated on three fundamental assumptions: Firstly, social arrangements crystallize expectations into coherent belief systems that can be attributed to both individuals and collectives; secondly, scholarly publications codify these expectations; and thirdly, academic disciplines naturally coalesce into organized networks centered around systematized expectations. While social institutions tend to be geographically localized, academic discourse transcends borders, allowing expectations to become standardized and disseminated globally through specialized communication frameworks. This intellectual metamorphosis, however, operates beneath the surface as a subtle, hidden process accessible only to those who engage in conscious introspection. Nevertheless, the forming "zones of significance" exert a profound influence on continuously evolving social institutions by imposing constraints on what developments might ensue next. I propose harnessing incursive and hyper-incursive mathematical formulations derived from anticipatory systems theory to represent these potential trajectories. By running computational simulations of these equations, we can examine how the recursive advancement of social structures is linked to the hyper-incursive transformation of expectation networks at the institutional scale, and how incursive realization of expectations manifests in behavior, institutions, and written output.
label: ai

text: In this groundbreaking study, we delve into the concept of honing theory, which revolutionizes our understanding of creativity as the driving force behind cultural evolution. According to this paradigm, creativity arises from the intricate interplay between cognitive systems, which dynamically reorganize and recalibrate in response to environmental stimuli to minimize entropy. The creative process commences when individuals encounter ambiguous and mentally stimulating material, triggering a iterative exploration of diverse perspectives until sufficient cognitive reorganization takes place and mental arousal subsides. This reorganization is facilitated by neural alignment and flexible binding mechanisms, often augmented by temporarily adopting more fluid and associative thinking patterns. When creative outputs resonate with others, they catalyze cultural advancements, leading to increasingly sophisticated and nuanced understandings of the world. Honing theory posits that cultural evolution occurs within self-organizing cognitive systems, with creative outputs serving as milestones of their evolutionary journey. By addressing the lacunae in existing creativity theories, honing theory provides a distinctive explanatory framework, substantiated by behavioral investigations, agent-based simulations, and conceptual merging models.
label: ai

text: We unveil a pioneering suite of problem solvers that harness the power of self-referential logic to tackle any arbitrary challenge. Inspired by Kurt Gödel's groundbreaking work on self-referential equations in 1931, these innovative solvers dynamically adapt their programming code in response to emerging evidence of potential improvements. Each problem domain is paired with a unique utility metric, hardware specifications, and starting code, all encapsulated within an initial proof-searching framework that is itself part of the starting code. By exhaustively exploring computable proof methods, the solvers seek out the most effective approach for self-modification, ultimately achieving global optimization without local plateaus. Unlike traditional methods reliant on predetermined proof-searching systems, our approach not only attains theoretically optimal complexity but also eliminates hidden performance limitations masked by big-O notation, assuming such enhancements can be proven advantageous in principle.
label: ai

text:
In 1976, Aumann's groundbreaking finding showed that when rational Bayesians share the same initial beliefs, their differing opinions will inevitably converge to a single consensus once their perspectives are openly discussed. Despite extensive research on the conditions necessary for this theorem, two critical questions have yet to be thoroughly addressed: can agents realistically achieve consensus through a prolonged conversation, and can the required computational steps be efficiently executed? This study provides affirmative answers to both queries, thereby solidifying Aumann's foundational insight.

We demonstrate that two agents starting from a shared prior can reach a consensus on the expected value of a variable ranging from 0 to 1, with high probability relative to their prior, by exchanging merely 1/epsilon^2 bits. Remarkably, this communication requirement is independent of the number of bits representing each agent's private information. Our analysis is then extended to settings with multiple agents, and we present a case study showcasing that the conventional approach used by economists, involving iteratively disclosing current expectations, nearly achieves this theoretical limit, while an alternative "attenuated protocol" outperforms it. Furthermore, we propose a method allowing two Bayesian reasoners to reach epsilon-agreement by exchanging 1/epsilon^2 messages, implementable by computationally bounded agents. Specifically, external observers examining the agents' information and communication record would find no discernible difference from idealized Bayesians. The computational cost of this implementation grows exponentially with 1/epsilon^6, yet remains independent of the number of agents involved.
label: ai

text: In pursuit of a theory of everything, we explore the probability distribution P that underlies the emergence of our universe's history. We propose that P can be formally described, despite the vast majority of distributions lacking such a characterization. Our analysis reveals that P remains negligible when the universe's history cannot be succinctly represented. We examine two competing distributions that define distinct theories of everything: one prioritizing concise constructive encoding, rooted in classical computability theory, and another emphasizing computational efficiency, informed by Levin's optimal search strategy and resource constraints. We identify a cumulative enumerable measure that reconciles these two distributions, assigning diminished probability to universes lacking efficient enumerating algorithms. Our findings have far-reaching implications for observer development, inductive inference, relativistic and quantum phenomena, philosophical questions, and the ultimate fate of our cosmos.
label: ai

text:
In the realm of theoretical computer science and database research, we propose a novel technique for approximating the pth frequency moments in evolving data streams, where 0<p<=2, by harnessing the power of asymmetric stable random projections. Our method outperforms existing symmetric approaches, achieving substantial gains as p approaches 1.

Our proposed technique can accommodate various data streams, including those with insertions only (the cash-register model), non-negative values throughout (the strict Turnstile model), or non-negative values at designated checkpoints. These constraints are minimal and realistic in practical applications.

We find that our method excels when p = 1+/- \Delta, with small \Delta values, which is particularly relevant in real-world scenarios such as modeling decay rates or interest rates. When \Delta = 0, computing the first frequency moment becomes nearly error-free through simple counting. Our approach can be viewed as an enhanced counting mechanism that can project future total values while incorporating decay or interest effects.

Our work consists of two primary contributions. Firstly, we introduce the concept of asymmetric stable random projections for this purpose. Secondly, we construct multiple statistical estimators tailored to asymmetric stable distributions, deriving their variance calculations, error bounds, and sample complexity requirements from fundamental principles.
label: ai

text: As modern warfare evolves, military operations rely increasingly on cutting-edge computational systems across all levels of command, from individual troops equipped with digital gear to leaders utilizing advanced decision-making tools and infrastructure. At its core, command and control is the harmonious integration of personnel, equipment, and logistics aimed at achieving strategic objectives through meticulous planning, constant oversight, and decisive direction. Given the proliferation of cyber warfare, technological advancements, information battlespace, and autonomous systems, command and control frameworks are undergoing a radical metamorphosis. Consequently, there is a pressing need for a framework that prioritizes adaptability, component reusability, maintainability, resilience, and seamless integration – enabling distant commanders to orchestrate, monitor, and direct battlefield activities with consistency, reliability, responsiveness, and efficacy. This work presents a service-oriented framework tailored to military arms systems and operational command infrastructure, comprising independent, geographically dispersed functional modules delivered via web-based technologies. The framework consists of three primary components: a client component encompassing all military computational infrastructure; a service component providing essential capabilities to client systems; and an integration component featuring an enterprise messaging layer facilitating coordination among interconnected system elements. A prototype command and control platform was built and evaluated, successfully demonstrating the feasibility of the proposed service-based approach. Future investigation could further enhance this framework by incorporating cryptographic safeguards to protect communication between system participants.

text: In a perturbative framework, a one-dimensional Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model reveals how the effective mass responds to changes in the adiabatic parameter $\omega_{\pi}/J$. Here, $\omega_{\pi}$ represents the phonon energy at the zone boundary and $J$ is the electron band hopping integral. Analysis of diagrams up to low order shows that two-phonon scattering events gain importance in the intermediate regime, where zone boundary phonons have energies comparable to those of band electrons. This intermediate regime is marked by the emergence of a polaronic transition, where the effective mass doubles compared to the bare band mass. Furthermore, the mass renormalization exhibits a sharp jump at $\omega_{\pi}\sim \sqrt{2}J$, attributed to the distinctive one-dimensional character of the dispersion relations. In contrast, electrons remain largely unaffected in both fully adiabatic and fully antiadiabatic limits.
label: ai

text: In a novel approach, we utilize diagrammatic perturbation theory to scrutinize the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model Hamiltonian in the presence of weak electron-phonon interactions in a one-dimensional setting. Our thorough examination of the carrier effective mass and electron spectral function reveals that electrons behave as distinct quasiparticles in both the adiabatic and antiadiabatic limits. Notably, when the energy scales of phononic and electronic degrees of freedom converge, characteristic phenomena emerge, marking the onset of the intermediate coupling regime. Within this intermediate region, we uncover a notable enhancement of the carrier mass and a dispersal of spectral weight across multiple transition features, signifying the increasing significance of many-phonon processes as electron-phonon coupling strengthens. Ultimately, the quasiparticle picture falters, and the system transitions into a polaron-like state.
label: ai

text: In a two-dimensional Su-Schrieffer-Heeger system, we investigate the equilibrium thermodynamic properties by adopting a path integral approach that accounts for the varying extent of electronic hopping events. By integrating out the lattice coordinates as classical variables and treating electronic trajectories quantum mechanically, we derive the system's free energy and its temperature-dependent derivatives. These are obtained by summing over the dominant path configurations at arbitrary temperatures, which dominate the partition function. Interestingly, at low temperatures, the heat capacity per temperature displays an anomalous rise reminiscent of glassy behavior, a phenomenon that becomes more pronounced on square lattices compared to one-dimensional chains. This is attributed to the increased contribution of the hopping potential to the total action in higher spatial dimensions.
label: ai

text: Despite ongoing debate, the notion that macroscopic patterns shape the evolutionary trajectory of living organisms remains a topic of contention. On one hand, prevailing wisdom suggests that evolution follows a directional path towards increasing complexity, yet empirical findings and theoretical examinations challenge this assumption. Through natural selection, organisms adapt to their environments, yet the process by which localized adaptation yields universal evolutionary patterns remains enigmatic. This study presents a simplified model that illustrates how adapting to a fluctuating habitat can give rise to a large-scale pattern of enhanced adaptability. According to the model, perpetual growth in adaptability necessitates a dynamic environment. Furthermore, the model reveals that perpetually increasing adaptability is linked to accelerated evolutionary change. I propose that unlimited adaptability expansion constitutes a fundamental macroscopic pattern in biological evolution, and explore empirically verifiable implications of the model for understanding how organisms evolve.

text: In a pioneering study, the application of monotone Kolmogorov complexity, defined by the formula Km = -log m, is explored for sequence forecasting purposes, grounded in the principles of universal deterministic and single-component minimum description length (MDL). The quantity m exhibits a remarkable similarity to Solomonoff's universal prior M, which is renowned for its predictive prowess across both deterministic and stochastic settings. However, the effectiveness of m in forecasting remains a topic of inquiry due to the scarcity of knowledge regarding the alignment of their posteriors—a crucial consideration for making predictions. Our research reveals that, in deterministic computable contexts, the "posterior" and associated losses of m converge; yet, this convergence is not uniform across all sequences, with some exhibiting rapid convergence while others may take a more leisurely pace. Conversely, in stochastic environments, neither the posterior nor the losses display convergence behavior.
label: ai

text: Within the Bayesian framework, inductive reasoning has been extensively developed and successfully applied to various tasks such as hypothesis testing, parameter estimation, forecasting, categorization, and predictive modeling. Nevertheless, traditional statistical methods often fall short in complex problem domains, failing to provide suitable model architectures and prior distributions. Building upon this foundation, Solomonoff's framework offers a mathematically rigorous and universally applicable approach to selecting model architectures and assigning priors. This discussion delves into the advantages of universal sequence forecasting, a method that transcends the i.i.d. assumption, resolving several theoretical and conceptual limitations inherent in conventional Bayesian sequential prediction techniques. Notably, Solomonoff's approach boasts robust point-in-time performance guarantees, sidesteps the zero posterior problem, and enjoys independence from parameterization and reparameterization choices, thereby addressing classic issues with incorporating prior knowledge and sequential adjustments. Moreover, this methodology proves effective and often superior in environments that exceed computational tractability.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering study, we explore the OR gate operation exhibited by a mesoscopic ring pierced by a magnetic flux $\phi$. The ring is connected to two semi-infinite metallic leads through symmetric interfaces, with control voltages $V_a$ and $V_b$ applied across one section of the ring, acting as the gate's dual inputs. Leveraging the tight-binding framework and Green's function formalism, we numerically compute conductance-energy and current-voltage relationships as functions of control voltages, ring-lead coupling parameters, and applied magnetic flux. Notably, our findings reveal that when $\phi$ equals half a flux quantum ($\phi_0=ch/e$), the device produces a substantial output current (logical 1) whenever at least one input assumes a high state (1), whereas both inputs being low (0) yields minimal output current (logical 0). This behavior straightforwardly realizes OR gate logic, opening up opportunities for the development of nanoscale electronic logic devices.
label: ai

text: Investigating electron flow through a benzene ring with multiple terminals, we expose the intricate dynamics of quantum transport under the influence of an Aharonov-Bohm magnetic flux. Employing a tight-binding approach within the Green's function framework, we elucidate the fundamental mechanisms governing electron flow across three terminals, shedding light on the interplay between molecular-lead coupling and magnetic flux. Our numerical simulations reveal a wealth of information, including multi-terminal conductances, reflection probabilities, and current-voltage relationships, which collectively underscore the remarkable transistor-like behavior exhibited by a benzene molecule coupled to three terminals. This phenomenon holds significant potential for nanoelectronic device engineering and establishes a solid theoretical foundation for understanding electron transport in complex multi-terminal quantum systems.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering study, we explore the behavior of a NOT logic gate fabricated using a mesoscopic ring threaded by a magnetic flux $\phi$. The ring is symmetrically connected to two infinite one-dimensional metallic leads, with a gate potential $V_a$ applied to one section of the ring serving as the input signal. Our analysis leverages the tight-binding formalism and Green's function techniques to numerically calculate conductance-energy relationships and current-voltage dependencies, parameterized by the coupling between the ring and leads, the magnitude of magnetic flux, and the applied gate potential. Our results reveal that when $\phi$ equals half the fundamental magnetic flux quantum ($\phi_0=ch/e$), the system exhibits a distinct response pattern, producing a significant output current when the input signal is weak (0) and a minimal output current when the input signal is strong (1). This NOT gate functionality suggests promising applications in the development of nanoscale electronic switching devices.
label: ai

text: In a pioneering study, researchers have delved into the intriguing phenomenon of electrons traversing zigzag-edged ribbons arranged in a honeycomb pattern, which are linked to two semi-infinite metallic leads. Employing the tight-binding formalism and Green's function methods, the team has conducted numerical simulations to elucidate the conductance behavior as a function of energy and the corresponding current-voltage relationships, which are governed by the ribbon dimensions. The results reveal that these honeycomb lattice ribbons, terminated with zigzag edges, invariably exhibit a conductance gap centered at E=0, which gradually narrows but remains persistent as the ribbon dimensions increase. This phenomenon underscores the inherent semiconducting nature of these structures, as exemplified by the current-voltage relationships that are reported herein.

text: In the realm of visual programming languages, diagrams play a pivotal role, enabling developers to illustrate both the structural components and control flow within code. By leveraging this approach, programmers can significantly enhance comprehension, streamline code maintenance, validate their work, ensure quality, and facilitate concurrent execution. This study presents a novel diagrammatic method capable of generating a visual representation that maps programming instructions to source code. Although the proposed technique holds immense potential for widespread adoption across various domains, the current investigation focuses on its application within the context of C++ programming. By employing this methodology to illustrate the fundamental elements of C++ programming, the research showcases its ability to provide a foundation for grasping how programs execute. Furthermore, testing this approach on actual C++ implementations reveals substantial improvements in learners' understanding of the computational processes that unfold when a C++ program runs.

text: In recent times, groundbreaking advancements in genetic engineering have given rise to an influx of novel engineered organisms. As engineered life becomes more pervasive, it is essential to develop strategies to distinguish engineered from naturally occurring organisms. This paper recasts the task of differentiation as an extension of the timeless inquiry tackled by Darwin in his seminal work, On the Origin of Species. By formalizing the question of tracing the origin of biological samples, we demonstrate that under certain conditions – namely, when originator algorithms operate without computational constraints and possess equivalent prior probabilities – the problem becomes fundamentally insoluble in its most general form. The significance of these findings extends to astrobiologists, researchers seeking to develop a comprehensive understanding of life itself, and those concerned with AI safety.
label: ai

text: A recent proliferation of metrics has revolutionized the assessment of journal quality, courtesy of the emergence of Scopus and Google Scholar, as well as advancements in Social Network Analysis. This study embarks on a comparative analysis of these novel metrics alongside their predecessors, aiming to discern whether distinct indicators capture unique aspects of citation patterns or exhibit significant correlations. Furthermore, it scrutinizes their reliability and temporal consistency. Two dominant themes emerge from this investigation: magnitude and significance, which collectively determine scholarly influence. The H-index seamlessly integrates these two components, functioning as a visibility metric akin to Indegree. PageRank primarily serves as a magnitude metric, although it demonstrates notable connections to network positioning metrics. Meanwhile, the Scimago Journal Ranking presents a rival to the traditional Journal Impact Factor, boasting a more complex underlying calculation.

text: In the realm of artificial intelligence, EVOC is a sophisticated simulation model that replicates the evolutionary process of human culture. By harnessing the power of neural networks, this innovative system gives rise to intelligent agents that not only conceptualize behaviors but also adopt actions from their neighboring counterparts. Notably, EVOC's unique fitness metric yields findings consistent with those of a precursor model, MAV, showcasing a steady increase in average action fitness and an initial surge followed by a decline in action diversity. A closer examination of the variables influencing diversity reveals a positive correlation with the number of needs, group size, group density, and the gradual erosion of boundaries between groups. Interestingly, a gentle weakening of boundaries fosters diversity by allowing for specialization and the dissemination of successful actions. Furthermore, rectangular environments seem to promote greater diversity compared to toroidal ones. However, when a dominant individual broadcasts its actions across the entire group, both action fitness and diversity tend to decline; this trend is mitigated as the number of dominant individuals increases. Finally, communities with sparse populations tend to generate less effective concepts, although broadcasting can help alleviate this limitation.

text: Two personality disorders, borderline and narcissistic, have garnered extensive scholarly focus and continue to be pivotal areas of study in clinical research. The prevailing psychoanalytic perspective on mental health centers on the coping strategies individuals employ to deflect distress. Concurrently, a prominent strand of inquiry investigates the role of psychological trauma and how dissociation serves as a means of coping. While each theoretical approach sheds light on distinct aspects of disordered psychological processes, they share considerable conceptual overlap. This study integrates both frameworks, introducing the Quadripolar Relational Model—a theoretical construct that situates mental functioning within the context of relational dynamics. By drawing upon principles from computational science, this model yields novel therapeutic strategies targeting both personality disorders and traumatic experiences.
